key_insight: Inherited and acquired deleterious mitochondrial DNA heteroplasmies are linked to higher risk of clonal hematopoiesis and hematological malignancy, suggesting a causal role in cancer development.
clinical_relevance: This work points to mtDNA heteroplasmy as a potential risk biomarker for blood cancer susceptibility and a possible avenue for prevention-focused risk stratification.
method: Analyzed ~36,000 first-degree relative pairs from UK Biobank and All of Us.
Separated shared heteroplasmies (inherited) from not shared heteroplasmies (de novo).
Used this framework to test whether deleterious mtDNA heteroplasmy predicts hematologic disease risk.
evidence_signal: Shared deleterious heteroplasmy was associated with hematological malignancies (RR=2.81, 95% CI 1.29-6.13) and showed concordant effects with non-shared burden, supporting causality in the abstracted analyses.
watch_item: Findings are associative and cohort-based, so clinical use would need independent validation, standardized heteroplasmy measurement, and careful control for ancestry and confounding.
paper_title: Deleterious mitochondrial heteroplasmy drives high-risk clonal hematopoiesis and hematological malignancy
